core_takeaway: The earlier formal position did not beat the last position because all five formal proposals converged to the same allocation before voting. There is some early anchoring around Agent_1 keeping Jewel, but the accepted last proposal duplicated the first proposal and produced no first-vs-last utility edge.
why_or_counterexample: As a counterexample to a clean first-position advantage, Proposal #1 and Proposal #5 contain the same allocation and every proposal received unanimous accept votes. The first speaker's Jewel claim helped shape the shared baseline, but by the proposal phase the last proposer could submit the identical consensus package and be accepted with equal substance.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 opened with ambitious two-item trade options, but public discussion converged on a one-item-per-agent baseline: Jewel to Agent_1, Stone to Agent_2, Pencil to Agent_3, Quill to Agent_4, Apple to Agent_5.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Present: five entries. Agents privately framed the same baseline as stable and consensus-friendly, with top or near-top items assigned to strong proponents and smaller items deferred for possible Round 2 adjustment.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: All five formal proposals were identical. The last proposal was selected after unanimous acceptance, but it did not improve on or differ from the first proposal.
phase_notes.private_voting: Present: 25 entries. Every private vote accepted; voters repeatedly cited identical allocation, secured top items, stable baseline logic, and avoidance of worse outcomes.

mechanisms.explanation: By the formal proposal phase, the first and last positions were substantively the same. The system selected Proposal #5, but that selection did not reflect a better last position or a worse first position.

mechanisms.theme: Early public anchoring helped Agent_1 keep Jewel
mechanisms.explanation: The main evidence for a possible first-position advantage is not the formal first proposal beating the last, but the way public discussion repeatedly preserved Agent_1's claim on Jewel.

mechanisms.theme: Top-item matching made the shared package easy to accept
mechanisms.explanation: The outcome was driven by each major claimant getting a salient high-value item, not by the first proposal outperforming the last. Agents voted for the package because it secured their own best or acceptable immediate item.

evidence.quote: locks Pencil with me (my top value)
Gives me Quill (52) in Round 1, my top priority
Allocates Apple to me (Agent_5) as my top priority

mechanisms.theme: Agent_2 accepted a smaller share to avoid downside risk
mechanisms.explanation: The one agent who did not receive a top item still accepted the consensus baseline because it provided immediate positive value and reduced negotiation risk. This made the identical last proposal viable rather than allowing the first proposal to dominate through conflict.

evidence.quote: may leave Agent_2 with a smaller share
reduce risk of ending with zero utility
avoiding the risk of a worse outcome
locks in a stable Round 1 arrangement

mechanisms.explanation: Repeated promises of a clean Round 2 adjustment path helped agents accept the current baseline quickly. That mechanism supports equality between first and last formal positions because all proposers used the same deferral logic.

evidence.quote: leaves a clean path for Round 2
leaves a clean path for Round 2 rebalances
